plan.contextChunk:
## Introduction

As objects move in the world, or as the camera moves, the projection of
the dynamic scene into the two-dimensional (2D) camera plane produces a
sequence of temporally varying pixel brightness. Before diving into how
to estimate motion from pixels, it is useful to understand the image
formation process. Studying how three-dimensional (3D) motion projects
into the camera will allow us to understand what the difference is
between a moving camera or a moving object and what types of constraints
one might be able to use to estimate motion.

## 3D Motion and Its 2D Projection {#d-motion-and-its-2d-projection}

A 3D point will follow a trajectory $\mathbf{P}(t) = (X(t),Y(t),Z(t))$,
in camera coordinates (@fig-optical_flow_basic_motion_point). As the
point moves, it has an instantaneous 3D velocity of
$\dot{\mathbf{P}} = (\dot{X}(t), \dot{Y}(t), \dot{Z}(t))$. The
projection of this point into the image plane location is
$\mathbf{p}(t) = (x(t),y(t))$ and its projection will move with the 2D
instantaneous velocity $\dot{\mathbf{p}} = (\dot{x}(t), \dot{y}(t))$,
where all the derivatives are done with respect to time, $t$.

![A 3D point, $\mathbf{P}$, moving in the world projects a 2D moving point, $\mathbf{p}$, into the camera plane.](figures/optical_flow/basic_motion_point.png){width="50%" #fig-optical_flow_basic_motion_point}

Using the equations of perspective projection $x=f X/Z$ and $y=f Y/Z$
(assuming that the camera is at the origin of the world-coordinate
system), we can derive the equations of how the instantaneous velocity
in the camera plane relates to the point motion in 3D world coordinates:
$$\begin{aligned}
\dot{x} &= f \frac{\dot{X} Z - \dot{Z} X}{Z^2} = \frac{f \dot{X} - \dot{Z} x}{Z}\\
\dot{y} &= f \frac{\dot{Y} Z - \dot{Z} Y}{Z^2} = \frac{f \dot{Y} - \dot{Z} y}{Z} 
\end{aligned}$$ The second expression is obtained by using $x=f \, X/Z$
and $y=f \, Y/Z$, which removes the dependency on the world coordinates
$X$ and $Y$. Note that $f$ is the focal length. In many derivations, the
notation is simplified by setting the focal length $f=1$. Here we will
keep it to make explicit which factors depend on the camera parameters
and which ones do not. We can write the last two equations in matrix
form as:

elements: 3D point P (large black sphere labeled P)
3D velocity vector dotP (red arrow at P, labeled dotP)
point O (black sphere labeled O at camera origin)
image plane (gray rectangle extending from O)
projected 2D point p (black sphere where projection ray meets plane, labeled p)
2D velocity vector dotp (red arrow at p, labeled dotp)
projection ray (solid green line from O to P, passing through p)
projection ray extension (dashed green line from p through image plane toward P)
labels for all points and vectors (O, P, p, dotP, dotp)

interactions.label: 3D Point Depth (Z)
interactions.teaches: Changing this demonstrates how the point's distance from the camera affects its 2D projection and the speed of its motion in the image.

interactions.label: Velocity Direction (theta)
interactions.teaches: This rotates the 3D velocity vector of the moving point, showing how different directions project into the camera plane.

interactions.label: Velocity Magnitude
interactions.teaches: This demonstrates how faster or slower 3D motion affects the observed 2D velocity in the image.

demo_steps.title: Point Motion at Fixed Depth
demo_steps.narration: Let's start with a 3D point at a medium distance from the camera. Its velocity points partly upward and to the right. You can see the projection ray connecting the 3D point and its image on the camera plane, as well as the projected 2D velocity vector. Notice how the 3D motion creates an observable shift in the image.

demo_steps.title: Bringing Point Closer
demo_steps.narration: Now, let's bring the 3D point much closer to the camera. Notice that the projected 2D motion on the camera plane becomes much larger and faster, even though the 3D speed hasn't changed. This illustrates how apparent motion can be amplified just by changing distance.

demo_steps.title: Changing 3D Velocity Direction
demo_steps.narration: Now, rotate the direction of the 3D velocity to point directly toward the camera. Watch how the 2D image motion shrinks. When all motion is along the view axis, the 2D projection moves more slowly. This is a crucial distinction—sideways motion projects strongly, but motion toward or away from the camera projects weakly.

demo_steps.title: Increasing 3D Velocity Magnitude
demo_steps.narration: Finally, increase the speed of the 3D point. Notice how the projected 2D velocity vector grows correspondingly. Both depth and real-world speed together determine how quickly the projection moves across the image.

interactionPlan.camera_suggestion: A slightly elevated diagonal view showing both the camera origin, the image plane, and the full projection ray. Fit all labeled points and vectors comfortably and allow rotation during user exploration.
interactionPlan.notes: Use strong color contrast for vectors (red for velocities, green for rays), transparent gray for the image plane, and large bold labels. Ensure vector arrows are always visible and update instantaneously with control changes.

attempts.html: ⚠ Figure failed to load

discrepancies: Arrowheads in the original are visually more prominent/large than in the generated figure.
The label positioning in the generated figure is less refined and slightly misaligned compared to the textbook figure (e.g. Pdot and pdot overlay arrows less clearly).
The green projection ray is dashed only from the image plane, but original renders this as brighter and more stylized.
The border of the image plane in the generated figure is uniform, while in the source figure it is thicker.
Sphere sizes and their relative proportions are slightly off compared to the original.
evaluation.notes: Faithfully recreates the main geometry and pedagogical intent with strong, pedagogical interactions; label and arrow stylings could be further refined.
action_items: Increase the relative size and boldness of red arrowheads to better match original emphasis.
Improve label positioning and alignment, especially aligning Pdot and pdot labels more clearly with their respective arrows.
Refine the image plane border and projection ray dash styles for closer visual fidelity.
Adjust sphere sizes and line thicknesses to proportionally match the textbook example.
Optionally fine-tune green color vibrancy for more vivid matching to original.

feedback.rationale: All issues are related to visual styling, alignment, and proportion details which should be addressed at the generation step, with no conceptual or structural problems noted.
action_items: Increase the relative size and boldness of red arrowheads to better match original emphasis.
Improve label positioning and alignment, especially aligning Pdot and pdot labels more clearly with their respective arrows.
Refine the image plane border and projection ray dash styles for closer visual fidelity.
Adjust sphere sizes and line thicknesses to proportionally match the textbook example.
Optionally fine-tune green color vibrancy for more vivid matching to original.
actionItems: Increase the relative size and boldness of red arrowheads to better match original emphasis.
Improve label positioning and alignment, especially aligning Pdot and pdot labels more clearly with their respective arrows.
Refine the image plane border and projection ray dash styles for closer visual fidelity.
Adjust sphere sizes and line thicknesses to proportionally match the textbook example.
Optionally fine-tune green color vibrancy for more vivid matching to original.

ẋ = 0.00, ẏ = 0.00
⚠ Figure failed to load

discrepancies: The original uses more dramatic 3D perspective effects on the plane and lines, which is subtler in the rendering.
The labels in the original are distinctly styled (bold, large, offset, with LaTeX-like characters); the generated version approximates but some fine typographic detail is lost.
The original figure's dashed green line is finer and offset, here it's chunkier and not as crisp.
The red velocity vectors are slightly different in scale and placement relative to their spheres compared to original.
The original's colors are more vivid/contrasty (especially green/red), while the render's coloring is a bit more muted.
evaluation.notes: Excellent interactive and conceptual match—minor visual and typographic differences from the reference.
action_items: Increase color saturation and contrast of red and green lines/arrows to better match the original.
Refine label typography and offset for LaTeX-like bold letters, and finesse label placement for exactness.
Tweak dashed line appearance to achieve a finer, more dashed look mimicking the original.
Slightly adjust the scaling and relative positioning of velocity arrows with respect to their spheres.

feedback.rationale: All necessary elements and interactions are present and correct; remaining issues are visual fidelity and details that require improvements in the rendering and styling.
action_items: Increase color saturation and contrast of red and green lines/arrows to better match the original.
Refine label typography and offset for LaTeX-like bold letters, and finesse label placement for exactness.
Tweak dashed line appearance to achieve a finer, more dashed look mimicking the original.
Slightly adjust the scaling and relative positioning of velocity arrows with respect to their spheres.

Refine label typography and offset for LaTeX-like bold letters, and finesse label placement for exactness.

discrepancies: In the 3D figure, the green dashed line representing the projection ray is not as visually distinct as the original (contrast/opacity issue).
Arrowheads in the 3D version are chunkier and less integrated with the shafts compared to the more refined arrows in the original.
Spheres appear slightly larger and less clearly distinguished, especially at the projected image point.
The relative proportions and positioning of the image plane versus the points are a bit off (plane appears bigger and more offset).
Label placements can intersect with geometry, making some areas visually cluttered compared to the original's cleaner layout.
evaluation.notes: Excellent interactive pedgogical illustration with rich controls and step-throughs, but there are minor visual and proportional differences and some label overlaps.
action_items: Adjust the opacity and width of the green dashed projection ray to make it visually closer to the original.
Refine the arrow geometry to make the arrowheads sleeker and better integrated with the shaft, matching the reference style.
Reposition critical labels to avoid visual clutter and overlapping geometry, emulating the label separation/placement from the source figure.
Tune plane and point size/proportions so the geometric relationships match the original more closely.
Slightly reduce the black spheres' radii and intensify contrast for projected points to improve figure clarity.

feedback.rationale: All action items concern implementation details like visual styling, label placement, and geometry adjustments, indicating the plan is sound but the generation needs refinement.
